nodes.label: AI as time-saving tool
nodes.grounding_span: I use AI only a couple to a few times per week. I usually use it because I run across a tedious task that is going to really slow me down otherwise.

nodes.label: AI reliability and accuracy in generated content
nodes.label_negative: AI inaccuracy and unreliability in content generation
nodes.grounding_span: the AI made a slide that was all about claims and was mixing up claims and arguments. I didn't notice right away, then had to explain to my students in class why my slide had inaccurate information in it.

nodes.label: AI as brainstorming and idea generator
nodes.grounding_span: When I use AI for an event company that I own, and not for teaching/research, I am much more collaborative, because I'm more focused on idea generation than time saving.

nodes.label: AI as equity tool for underserved students
nodes.label_negative: AI as elitist or exclusionary tool
nodes.grounding_span: Anything that makes old-school professors so unanimously skeptical, to me, is probably something that will help underserved students.

nodes.label: AI understanding of specific constraints
nodes.grounding_span: No matter how many times I explain that LARP isn't something where I can control the players or have them lose autonomy, ChatGPT always suggest plots where players would have to pretend to lose half of their memories

nodes.label: Efficiency and cost reduction
nodes.grounding_span: I could be doing something more requiring of my skillset instead.

nodes.label: Teaching accuracy and confidence
nodes.grounding_span: I can make sure more easily that inaccurate information doesn't end up on my slides if I'm the one generating the information.

nodes.label: Creative inspiration and ideation
nodes.grounding_span: I'm more focused on idea generation than time saving.

nodes.label: Equity and student support
nodes.grounding_span: I have, in fact, found that the time-saving elements of using AI for English classes does help students.

nodes.label: Satisfaction with AI as a helpful tool
nodes.grounding_span: I could totally do it myself, but it would take so much longer and I could be doing something more requiring of my skillset instead.

nodes.label: Frustration with AI inaccuracy in content creation
nodes.grounding_span: I felt particularly frustrated when it produced that slide I mentioned earlier.

nodes.label: Satisfaction with AI for lesson creation
nodes.grounding_span: It felt particularly satisfying when I was able to teach ESL students who were freshmen to talk to AI about their topics

nodes.label: Resistance to AI in creative work
nodes.grounding_span: Honestly, it's terrible at it. ... But, it usually gives me enough information that I am able to then pull out useful parts

nodes.label: Optimistic anticipation of deeper collaboration
nodes.grounding_span: I hope to be able to occasionally not need to just unload tasks on AI and to be able to collaborate more deeply.

nodes.label: Task-dependent delegation vs. collaboration
nodes.grounding_span: When I use AI for an event company that I own, and not for teaching/research, I am much more collaborative

nodes.label: Collaborative iterative refinement — works back and forth with AI
nodes.grounding_span: it usually gives me enough information that I am able to then pull out useful parts of the idea and ask more questions to flesh out the stories.
